summary: To minimize risks associated with Febrile Non-Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions during a blood transfusion, it’s essential to perform leukocyte removal or use irradiated red cells since they contain fewer white blood cell components which can trigger an immune response leading to symptoms like high body temperature post-transfusion. This process has been proven effective across various research findings due primarily to its ability to neutralize harmful inflammatory substances released into storage-stressed plasma. Agent2 concludes based on extensive literature review supporting leucodepletion techniques over other methods such as washing procedures given their superior effectiveness against known triggers of FNHTR mechanisms involving inflammation mediated via cytokine pathways originating within residual WBC populations present even after standard purification steps unless specifically targeted out using advanced filtration technologies aimed at achieving near-complete depletion prior administration back into patients undergoing therapeutic intervention requiring allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation among others where compatibility concerns necessitate stringent quality control measures including rigorous pathogen elimination protocols beyond mere antigen matching alone sufficient but not necessary conditions ensuring safe outcomes achievable only when combined judiciously alongside appropriate supportive care regimens tailored individually according patient needs assessed
